lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Will not King Richard let me speak with him?

lines.speaker: SHERIFF
lines.text: No, my good lord; therefore be patient.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Hastings, and Edward’s children, Rivers, Grey,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Holy King Henry, and thy fair son Edward,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Vaughan, and all that have miscarried

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: By underhand corrupted foul injustice,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: If that your moody discontented souls

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Do through the clouds behold this present hour,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Even for revenge mock my destruction!

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This is All-Souls’ day, fellows, is it not?

lines.speaker: SHERIFF
lines.text: It is, my lord.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Why, then All-Souls’ day is my body’s doomsday.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This is the day that, in King Edward’s time,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: I wish’d might fall on me, when I was found

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: False to his children or his wife’s allies;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This is the day wherein I wish’d to fall

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: By the false faith of him I trusted most;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: This, this All-Souls’ day to my fearful soul

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Is the determined respite of my wrongs:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: That high All-Seer that I dallied with

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Hath turn’d my feigned prayer on my head

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: And given in earnest what I begg’d in jest.

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Thus doth he force the swords of wicked men

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: To turn their own points on their masters’ bosoms:

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Now Margaret’s curse is fallen upon my head;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: “When he,” quoth she, “shall split thy heart with sorrow,

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Remember Margaret was a prophetess.”

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Come, sirs, convey me to the block of shame;

lines.speaker: BUCKINGHAM
lines.text: Wrong hath but wrong, and blame the due of blame.

stage_directions.text: Enter the Sheriff, and Buckingham, with halberds, led to execution.

glosses.definition: secretly corrupted, foul injustice — Buckingham's indictment of Richard's regime
laugh at my downfall — Buckingham invokes his victims as spectators
2 November — the feast commemorating all the faithful departed; historically Buckingham's actual execution date, 1483

glosses.phrase: All-Souls’ day is my body’s doomsday
glosses.definition: the day of the dead is the day of my death — Buckingham's theological pun
wished might befall me — Buckingham's remembered self-curse (cf. 2.1.32–40) now returning
the appointed reckoning for my crimes — 'respite' = delay before payment due
pretended prayer — Buckingham's false 2.1 reconciliation oath

glosses.phrase: given in earnest what I begg’d in jest
glosses.definition: granted seriously what I begged playfully — the god of 2.1's oath takes him at his word

glosses.phrase: Margaret’s curse is fallen upon my head
glosses.definition: second confirmation of Margaret's 1.3 prophecies (cf. Grey at 3.3.15)
quoting Margaret's own words back to her — Buckingham completes her curse's structure
wrong is paid only with wrong — Buckingham's closing moral arithmetic

annotations.title: Buckingham's All-Souls speech and the closure of the curse-chain
annotations.body: Buckingham's last soliloquy is the play's most precisely calibrated moment of curse-recognition. He is executed on All-Souls' Day — 2 November, the feast of the dead — and the date was both historical fact and dramaturgical gift. The speech works on three registers simultaneously. First, he recalls the elaborate self-curse he swore in 2.1 during Edward's forced reconciliation ("When I am cold in zeal to yours") and names it as now executed against him. Second, he closes Margaret's 1.3 curse-chain by quoting her directly: "'When he,' quoth she, 'shall split thy heart with sorrow, / Remember Margaret was a prophetess.'" Third, he articulates the play's theology of providence: "Thus doth he force the swords of wicked men / To turn their own points on their masters' bosoms." The speech is the clearest statement in the play of what the curses have meant. Buckingham dies understanding the system he enabled.
sources: A. P. Rossiter, Angel with Horns (1961)
Wolfgang Clemen, A Commentary on Shakespeare's Richard III (1968)
Bernard Spivack, Shakespeare and the Allegory of Evil (1958)

annotations.title: All-Souls' Day as fact and metaphor
annotations.body: Buckingham was in fact executed on All-Souls' Day, 2 November 1483, in Salisbury market square. The historical coincidence gives the scene its layered theological weight. All Souls (from the medieval tradition established c. 998 at Cluny) was the day set aside for prayer for all the faithful departed — particularly those in purgatory. Buckingham's "All-Souls' day is my body's doomsday" plays on the feast's day-of-reckoning connotation while acknowledging what the chronicles record. Shakespeare's audiences — still living inside decades of post-Reformation religious argument — would have heard the All-Souls reference with particular attention. The speech lets a Catholic calendar-detail carry a play's providential argument without committing the play to any specific doctrinal position. The dead, on the day of the dead, come for their witness.
sources: Eamon Duffy, The Stripping of the Altars (1992)

trials.prompt: On what date does Buckingham's execution take place, and why does the play emphasize it?
options: All-Souls' Day (2 November), the medieval feast for prayer for the faithful departed — the day-of-reckoning connotation gives the scene its theological weight (and matches historical fact)
St. George's Day — England's patron saint
Christmas Eve — a date Shakespeare invented for dramatic effect

trials.prompt: Buckingham's line "Thus doth he force the swords of wicked men / To turn their own points on their masters' bosoms" articulates what?
options: A military strategy for turning enemy weapons
The play's providential theology: God uses the wicked's own instruments against them — the clearest statement of what the curse-chain has meant

trials.prompt: Whose earlier curse does Buckingham explicitly quote back in his final speech?
options: The Duchess of York's curse on Richard
Margaret's 1.3 warning to Buckingham himself: "Remember Margaret was a prophetess"
